hero_image.caption: Booker T. Washington.
hero_image.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (digital ID hec.16114)
hero_image.license: Public domain (U.S. federal government work, 17 U.S.C. 105; also pre-1931)

inline_images.alt: Hampton Institute students constructing a house, 1899
inline_images.caption: Hampton Institute students at work on a house they largely built themselves, photographed by Frances Benjamin Johnston in 1899, the kind of trades-based industrial education Booker T. Washington championed.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (LCCN 00652583)
inline_images.license: Public domain (published before 1929; no known restrictions on publication)

related_chapters.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
related_chapters.part_title: Tuskegee and a Black Officer's War
related_chapters.chapter_title: Up at Four to Milk the Cows
related_chapters.why: Washington built Tuskegee on students working dairies and trades to fund and learn, and Bassett describes that exact work-study labor as a Tuskegee student.

related_chapters.subject: Clarence Magee
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Father Who Vowed His Children Would Read
related_chapters.why: Washington grounds racial advance in education paired with self-sufficiency, and Magee recounts a sharecropper father who tied literacy to the family's independence.

related_chapters.subject: Charles McLaurin
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Grandmother's Books and Booker T. Washington
related_chapters.why: This chapter names Booker T. Washington directly as McLaurin recounts a grandmother's books shaping him, the schooling-and-self-improvement creed the essay advances.

related_chapters.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
related_chapters.why: Washington argues an economic foundation precedes other gains, and Derby describes building craft cooperatives for Black economic development in Mississippi.

related_chapters.subject: Eddie Holloway
related_chapters.why: Washington holds that learning is the lever that lifts a people, and Holloway frames education itself as the route to freedom.

related_chapters.subject: Harold K. Brown
related_chapters.chapter_title: How Much Did Your Shoes Cost
related_chapters.why: Washington urges thrift and capital-building over display, and Brown's reflection on a generation's misplaced spending priorities runs along the same line.

top_voice.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
top_voice.reason: Bassett embodies Washington's Tuskegee model carried to its furthest end: a Tuskegee work-study student who rose through hands-on science to a doctorate in dairy technology.

closing_image.alt: SNCC broadside recruiting volunteers for the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer Project
closing_image.caption: SNCC broadside recruiting volunteers for the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer Project. Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett is the corpus voice this essay connects to most.
closing_image.photographer: Mississippi Department of Archives and History (institutional scan; original broadside printer unknown)
closing_image.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Flickr Commons)
closing_image.license: No known copyright restrictions (Flickr Commons designation)

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions Industrial Education for the Negro raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Harold K. Brown, Clifford D. Browner Jr., and Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner).

nearest_voices.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)
